Text encoding: Jonathan Prag, edition source:

Both Castelli and Gualtherus note that the stone had suffered significant damage to the letters. Agustín and Gualtherus report a continuous text with no gaps, but Castelli suggests that two whole lines are missing both before and after the third surviving line of text.
No reliable description of the letter forms is preserved.
193 BCE (if identification of L. Scipio Asiagenus accepted)
Badian, 1967, Review of Wilson, A.J.N. Emigration from Italy in the Republican Age of Rome
Brennan, 2000, The Praetorship in the Roman Republic
Di Vita, 1963, Il miliario siciliano del console C. Aurelio Cotta

Castelli, 1769, Siciliae et objacentium insularum veterum inscriptionum nova collectio
Castelli, 1784, Siciliae et objacentium insularum veterum inscriptionum nova collectio prolegomenis et notis illustrata, et iterum cum emendationibus, & auctariis evulgata
Funerary inscription for Marcus Limbricius and his wife Helvia Arura
Latin text preserved in full over four lines, vertically centred on the stone, with a vacat below. The first line is the largest, the third line smallest and most compressed.

The letters are large and simply cut, without serifs. M has hastae of equal length, all off-vertical; S is shallow; R is closed with a long tail extending from the eye; B has small upper eye, closed; V is wide; F has shorter lower bar; E has equal short bars; C is not perfectly curved/circular, but rather shallow. Interpuncts throughout, formed of light vertical strokes.
Antiquarium e sito archeologico di Halaesa
Photo J. Prag courtesy Soprintendenza BBCCAA di Messina
Gualtherus, 1624, Siciliae obiacentium insular et Bruttiorum antiquae tabulae cum animadversionibus Georgii Gualtheri

funerary inscription for marcus limbricius and his wife helvia arura
antiquarium e sito archeologico di halaesa
Nine lines of Greek text are preserved, almost complete, unevenly centred on the stone.
The letters are neatly and regularly cut, and of standard Hellenistic form, without serifs. Epsilon is notable for having a detached middle bar; beta is not closed.
Photo J.Prag courtesy Soprintendenza BBCCAA di Messina
L'année épigraphique: revue des publications épigraphiques relatives a l'antiquité romaine., 1888-
Manganaro, 1996, Alla ricerca di poleis mikrai della Sicilia centro-orientale
Pinzone, 2011, L'iscrizione alesina di Caninius Niger e il problema dei socii navales Siciliani
Burgio, 2012, Appendice. Monumenti minori dell'agora di Alesa: le esedre curve. Analisi e ricostruzione
Funerary honours for Aulus Mevius Zethus
Two lines of Latin text filling the available field. The second line is slightly indented (and so centred?).

Neatly engraved and well proportioned letters with modest serifs. M has hastae slightly off vertical; E has bars of equal length; Z has long and curving horizontal strokes; the third T in line 2 is extended above the line. Words are separated by comma-like triangular interpuncts which find frequent parallels in the inscriptions of Halaesa.
2nd century CE, if Manganaro's link with  is accepted.
Manganaro, 1989, Iscrizioni Latine nuove e vecchie della Sicilia
funerary honours for aulus mevius zethus
Dedication by a sevir to Concordia
The epigraphic face is preserved for a width of 12.5 cm, with two lines of Latin letters filling the available field.

The letters are tall, neatly cut and closely spaced (height c.30mm), and words are separated by simple triangular interpuncts. The letters are lightly serifed. E is tall and narrow, T has a curved cross-bar, rising to the right.
Late 1st century CE, or 2nd century CE
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Soprintendenza di Messina
dedication by a sevir to concordia
Honours for L. Naevius(?) Firminus Manilianus
Remains of four lines of latin text are preserved: only the lower part of the first line survives, and only traces of the tops of letters remain from the beginning of line four.

The letters are V-cut and generously (and sometimes unevenly) spaced, with moderate serifs. R is closed, and both A and R have lightly cut horizontals; all the strokes of M are off-vertical. Words are separated by comma-like interpuncts.
2nd century CE or early 3rd century CE
honours for l. naevius(?) firminus manilianus
The first part of all six lines of the Greek text are preserved, but none are complete.

The letters are uniform and well cut, with pronounced serifs. A hollow triangular interpunct is used between all the onomastic elements (but apparently not elsewhere in the text; spaces are however observed between words, with or without interpunct). Theta has a detached middle bar; omicron is full size; epsilon has a detached short middle bar; mu reaches almost to the lower line in the middle, and the outer strokes are vertical.
Moretti, 1986-1987, Per la storia di Halaesa in Sicilia
Campagna, 2007, Architettura pubblica ed evergetismo nella Sicilia di età repubblicana
Fragmentary latin inscription mentioning a proconsul

Letters are 50mm high, tall, thin, serifed. P and R are open; the hastae of M join early and extend upwards; a hedera like interpunct (tail top and bottom to a hollow element) appears once between C and V of line 2.
fragmentary latin inscription mentioning a proconsul
Cadastral inscription for the territory of Halaesa

2nd — 1st century BCE (Kaibel simply 'post-Roman conquest'; Calderone argues 2nd — 1st on letter forms etc. of his frag.; but, 1998, for c. 300 BCE, which Nenci too believes; Manganaro 2nd century BCE).

cadastral inscription for the territory of halaesa
soprintendenza beni culturali e ambientali di messina
Halaesa honours Diogenes Lapiron, son of Diogenes
Five lines of Greek text are preserved, with some of the initial letters lost at the left margin.
The letters are plainly and evenly cut, and well spaced, without serifs. Epsilon has a short middle bar, omicron is full size, omega is open.
The text cannot be precisely dated, but the style of the lettering suggests that it belongs to the 2nd century BCE, rather than the 1st.
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Museo Archeologico Regionale Antonino Salinas
halaesa honours diogenes lapiron, son of diogenes
Soldiers serving at Eryx honour their commander
Greek text is visible over 10 lines, although much of the initial lines in particular is now lost
Where visible, the letters are plainly and regularly cut, without serifs. Epsilon has short middle bar, omicron is full size, omega is closed and slightly elliptical
Late Hellenistic (period of the Roman Republican province)
Prestianni Giallombardo, 1987, Un'inedita iscrizione tardoantica da Alesa e il problema dell'episcopato alesino
soldiers serving at eryx honour their commander
Halaesa honours Gaius Vergilius Balbus, proquaestor
Badian, 1983, The silence of Norbanus. A note on provincial Quaestors under the Republic
Prag, 2007, Auxilia and gymnasia: a Sicilian model of Roman Republican Imperialism
halaesa honours gaius vergilius balbus, proquaestor
The text is recorded in the antiquarian tradition over 6 lines
No description exists, but Castelli 1784 transcribes with lunate sigma and mostly lunate epsilon
Copy of Castelli 1784, 192 cl.14 no.113 no.297 (from Arachne edition)
The text is presented over two lines in the transcription
In the transcription omega and sigma are lunate, with the sigma made up of three right straight strokes at right-angles; the first two letters are placed one above the other.
Detail of Agustín transcription from Prestianni 1987, p.302
Probably later 2nd century BCE on archaeological grounds
Block A: Photo J. Prag, courtesy Parco archeologico di Tindari
Carettoni, 1876-, Notizie degli scavi di antichità
The beginning of five lines of Greek letters more or less aligned with the left margin of the stone.
The letters are regular and well spaced. Alpha has broken bar; epsilon has a shorter middle bar.
Manganaro, 1999, Sikelika: studi di antichità e di epigrafia della Sicilia greca
Text encoding: Davide Massimo, edition source:

A single line of latin letters occupies the field between the mouldings on the front face; the field is 3.4 cm high x 15.3 cm wide. There is a vacat before the text, and a recessed space after, the surface of the stone being lost.
Plain Latin capitals, with minimal serifs; S is fairly tall and shallow; A curves slightly towards the feet; C sits slightly above the line; R is closed, with curving tail.
Parts of two lines of Latin letters are preserved set between guidelines top and bottom.

Regular v-cut letters with small serifs: E has equal length bars; M has all bars off vertical and the mid-point reaches down to the line; R is closed, with the tail extending off the eye; interpunct formed in the style of a comma
Remains of three lines of Latin letters.
Assumed to be Imperial in date from the use of Latin in comparison to other material from the site.
